title: Wave Frequency & Wavelength Calculator 2026 | Hacé Cuentas
h1: Calculate Wave Frequency, Wavelength & Velocity (v = λ × f)
description: Calculate wave velocity, frequency, or wavelength using v = λf. Enter two values, get the third. Works for sound, light, and radio. Free & instant.
intro: The fundamental wave relationship is **v = λ × f**, where v is the speed of propagation, λ (lambda) is the wavelength, and f is the frequency. This applies to all wave types: sound, light, radio waves, microwaves, and more. This calculator solves for any of the three variables.
keyTakeaway: **v = λ × f** → Visible light (λ ≈ 550 nm, f ≈ 545 THz) travels at **3×10⁸ m/s**. A 440 Hz musical note (A4) has λ ≈ **0.78 m** in air at 20°C.
useCases: Solve wave problems in physics and acoustics
Calculate wavelengths of radio or WiFi signals

faq.a: The distance between two consecutive equivalent points on a wave (for example, crest to crest). Measured in meters and symbolized as **λ** (lambda). Shorter wavelengths correspond to higher frequencies at the same speed.

faq.q: Does this work for light waves?
faq.a: Yes. Light travels at **c = 3×10⁸ m/s** (speed of light in vacuum). Visible light ranges from λ = 380 nm (violet) to 700 nm (red). For precise calculations, use 299,792,458 m/s.

faq.q: What is the wavelength of WiFi?
faq.a: WiFi 2.4 GHz: **λ ≈ 12.5 cm**. WiFi 5 GHz: **λ ≈ 6 cm**. WiFi 6 GHz: **λ ≈ 5 cm**. Longer wavelengths (2.4 GHz) penetrate walls better than shorter wavelengths.

faq.q: What is the frequency of the musical note A4?
faq.a: A4 (the 'A' above middle C) is **440 Hz**. In air at 20°C (343 m/s), its wavelength is approximately **0.78 m** (78 cm).

faq.q: What's the difference between Hz, kHz, MHz, and GHz?
faq.a: 1 kHz = 1,000 Hz; 1 MHz = 1,000,000 Hz; 1 GHz = 1,000,000,000 Hz. FM radio: ~88–108 MHz. Cell phones: ~700 MHz–6 GHz. Microwave ovens: ~2.45 GHz.

faq.q: Does the speed of sound change?
faq.a: Yes. In air at 20°C: **343 m/s**. In water: ~1,480 m/s. In steel: ~5,960 m/s. Sound travels faster in denser media and warmer air.

The full range of electromagnetic waves, ordered by frequency: Radio → Microwave → Infrared → **Visible light** → Ultraviolet → X-rays → Gamma rays. Visible light occupies only a tiny band in the middle.

faq.q: Why do subwoofers need to be so large?
faq.a: Bass frequencies (20–200 Hz) have very long wavelengths — a 20 Hz wave is about 17 meters long in air. Large speaker cones are needed to generate these long wavelengths efficiently.

faq.q: How do I convert frequency to wavelength?
faq.a: Use λ = v / f. Divide the wave's speed of propagation by its frequency. For example, a 100 Hz sound wave in air (343 m/s) has λ = 343 / 100 = **3.43 meters**.

explanation:
## The Fundamental Wave Equation

The relationship **v = λ · f** connects velocity, wavelength, and frequency for any wave—mechanical or electromagnetic. If you know two of the three variables, you can solve for the third:

- **v = λ · f** (velocity = wavelength × frequency)
- **λ = v / f** (wavelength = velocity ÷ frequency)
- **f = v / λ** (frequency = velocity ÷ wavelength)

## Typical Wave Speeds

| Wave Type | Speed (approximate) |
|---|---|
| Sound in air (20°C) | 343 m/s |
| Sound in water | 1,480 m/s |
| Sound in steel | 5,960 m/s |
| Light in vacuum | 299,792,458 m/s |
| Light in fiber optic cable | ~200,000,000 m/s |

## Common Frequency Ranges

| Type | Frequency |
|---|---|
| Human hearing | 20 Hz – 20 kHz |
| FM radio | 88 – 108 MHz |
| WiFi (2.4 GHz band) | 2,400 MHz |
| Visible light | 400 – 790 THz |
| Cell phone signal | 700 MHz – 6 GHz |

## Key Tips & Common Mistakes

- **Use consistent units**: If velocity is in m/s, wavelength must be in meters and frequency in Hz. Convert first if you have km/s or MHz.
- **Frequency doesn't change between media**: When a wave enters a new material (e.g., light entering water), frequency stays the same, but velocity and wavelength both change.
- **Medium matters**: Sound doesn't travel in a vacuum; light does. Propagation speed depends entirely on the medium.
- **Real-world context**: Audible sound has wavelengths from ~17 m (20 Hz, very low bass) to ~1.7 cm (20 kHz, high treble). This is why bass speakers are large and tweeters are small.
- For orbital periods and planetary motion, see our [Kepler's Third Law calculator](/kepler-third-law).

example.title: Real Example: Wavelength of A4 (440 Hz)
steps: **Given**: Frequency f = 440 Hz (musical note A4), velocity of sound v = 343 m/s (air at 20°C).

**Interpretation**: Each complete cycle of the A4 note occupies about 78 centimeters of air. This is why bass instruments (much lower frequencies, longer wavelengths) require larger resonance chambers.
example.result: The note A4 (440 Hz) has a wavelength of approximately 0.78 meters in air at 20°C.

sources.name: NIST — National Institute of Standards and Technology
